svg.title: MN 95 Caṅkī Sutta — With Caṅkī
svg.desc: A discourse visualization of MN 95: the brahmin dogma resting on a row of blind men, the five grounds that turn out two ways, the three movements of truth (preservation, awakening, final arrival), the three gates of examining a teacher, and the gradual cascade from faith to striving culminating in the final arrival at the truth.

g.text: “This alone is true, anything else is wrong” — but on what ground?
The brahmins debate who should visit whom; all go to the Buddha, and a young brahmin puts the question
Kāpaṭhika Bhāradvāja — sixteen, master of the three Vedas — asks:
“The ancient hymns of the brahmins, handed down through oral tradition, preserved in scripture —
on them the brahmins conclude: ‘This alone is true, anything else is wrong.’ What does sir Gotama say?”
itihitihaparamparāya piṭakasampadāya … ekaṁsena niṭṭhaṁ gacchanti: ‘idameva saccaṁ, moghamaññaṁ’
THE ROW OF BLIND MEN · ANDHAVEṆŪPAMĀ
“The first one does not see, the middle one does not see, the last one does not see.”
purimopi na passati, majjhimopi na passati, pacchimopi na passati
So the brahmins’ faith turns out to be without foundation — amūlikā saddhā
FIVE GROUNDS, TWO OUTCOMES · PAÑCA DHAMMĀ DVEDHĀ VIPĀKĀ
Whatever is fully accepted may yet be empty, hollow, false; what is not accepted may yet be factual, true, not otherwise
none of these settles whether a thing is true
Acceptance of a view after consideration
Acceptance need not make it true; rejection need not make it false
How truth is preserved: speak honestly of your ground, without leaping to a final verdict
One states honestly: “Thus is my faith” — likewise of inclination, oral tradition, reasoning, and view
… yet does not come to a definite conclusion: “This alone is true.” Thus, there is preservation of truth.
Preservation orients honesty; awakening verifies and realizes; final arrival completes through cultivation
AWAKENING: THE THREE GATES OF EXAMINATION
Before placing faith, a householder examines a teacher in three domains — tīsu dhammesu samannesati
Examine for anything that could distort the ability of a teacher to know and see.
Found pure, his conduct and his profound, subtle Dhamma (gambhīro … atakkāvacaro) cannot come from one so affected
→ Then he places faith in him · saddhaṁ niveseti
THE GRADUAL CASCADE · FROM FAITH TO STRIVING
Each step is the proximate support for the next — striving is most helpful for the final arrival at the truth
for the final arrival at the truth
Realizing the highest truth with the body, penetrating it with wisdom
